coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of heteroatom doped and undoped graphene quantum dots as active materials for reversible lithium and sodium ions storage
coredata.dc:creator: Vijaya Kumar Saroja, Ajay Piriya
Garapati, Meenakshi Seshadhri
ShyiamalaDevi, R.
Kamaraj, M.
Ramaprabhu, S.
coredata.dc:description: Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots have attractive properties but the synthesis of graphene quantum dots in a simple and scalable technique is tedious, which limits its utilization in different energy storage application. In this study, we present a simple and scalable approach to produce graphene quantum dots and heteroatom doped graphene quantum dots using chemical vapor deposition technique. Graphene quantum dots are prepared using alloy-based catalyst and methane as a carbon source. Boron-doped and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots are prepared at low temperature using graphite oxide without the use of dialysis bag. Here, the electrochemical lithium and sodium ion storage properties of doped and undoped graphene quantum dots are studied without being used as a supporting material for the performance enhancement as reported in previous reports. Boron doped GQD (B-GQD) exhibits a high specific capacity of 1097 mAh g−1 and 310 mAh g−1 at a specific current of 50 mA g−1 for lithium and sodium ion batteries respectively. B-GQD exhibits high volumetric energy density of 537 Ah L−1 and 214 Ah L−1 with an average voltage of 0.43 V and 0.57 respectively for lithium ion and sodium ion batteries. Also, the cells observe a satisfactory cyclic performance for 500 cycles with good capacity retention. Detailed investigations show that the edge defects present in GQD and doped GQDs help to enhance the electrochemical storage performance of lithium and sodium ions.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of heteroatom doped and undoped graphene quantum dots as active materials for reversible lithium and sodium ions storage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots have attractive properties but the synthesis of graphene quantum dots in a simple and scalable technique is tedious, which limits its utilization in different energy storage application. In this study, we present a simple and scalable approach to produce graphene quantum dots and heteroatom doped graphene quantum dots using chemical vapor deposition technique. Graphene quantum dots are prepared using alloy-based catalyst and methane as a carbon source. Boron-doped and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots are prepared at low temperature using graphite oxide without the use of dialysis bag. Here, the electrochemical lithium and sodium ion storage properties of doped and undoped graphene quantum dots are studied without being used as a supporting material for the performance enhancement as reported in previous reports. Boron doped GQD (B-GQD) exhibits a high specific capacity of 1097 mAh g−1 and 310 mAh g−1 at a specific current of 50 mA g−1 for lithium and sodium ion batteries respectively. B-GQD exhibits high volumetric energy density of 537 Ah L−1 and 214 Ah L−1 with an average voltage of 0.43 V and 0.57 respectively for lithium ion and sodium ion batteries. Also, the cells observe a satisfactory cyclic performance for 500 cycles with good capacity retention. Detailed investigations show that the edge defects present in GQD and doped GQDs help to enhance the electrochemical storage performance of lithium and sodium ions.

ce:section.ce:para: The fascinating carbon-based materials like graphite, graphene, carbon nanotubes and fullerene have received an intensive attention in various research fields. Among these materials, graphene is of great interest in the scientific community because of its two-dimensional structure, excellent electrical, mechanical, thermal and optical properties and therefore has been applied in various energy storage, conversion-based devices and membrane technology for desalination [1,2,11,12,3–10]. Due to the high theoretical capacity of graphene, it is investigated as an anode in lithium ion battery. On the other hand, the large specific surface area of graphene causes huge irreversible capacity in the initial cycle making it unfeasible to be used in practical application. Also, One-dimensional graphene quantum dots exhibits low energy density which restricts its use as active material in secondary batteries [13]. Also, it is observed that restacking of graphene is a serious issue due to the reduction of functional groups in the subsequent cycles when used as an electrode [14]. Luminescent carbon-based materials like graphene quantum dots and carbon dots are considered as emerging zero-dimensional carbon-based materials with unique physical and chemical properties [15,16]. The presence of edge effect and the confinement of sp2 carbon results in the opening of band gap thereby give rise to excellent optical properties [17]. When graphene sheets are fragmented to a size below the exciton Bohr radius (20 nm) graphene quantum dots are formed. Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are the class of zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures and the electronic property of the graphene quantum dots can be tuned by controlling the size of the particles. Graphene quantum dots have physical and chemical properties like photoluminescence, biocompatibility and low toxicity because of the quantum confinement effect and edge effect [18–20]. These properties allow using these graphene quantum dots in a widespread application like biosensors [21], catalysis [22–25], photonics [21] and photovoltaics [26,27].The use of these properties in energy storage application is limited. Chao et al. used GQD that are coated on the surface of VO2 which acts as the surface sensitizer to boost the electrochemical performance [28]. Also, GQD is used along with MoS2 to improve the electrochemical property in lithium-ion battery (LIB) [29]. Graphene oxide quantum dots are used as an additive in the gel polymer electrolyte which helps to suppress the formation of ion-solvent clusters thereby improves the lithium ion mobility and hence high lithium ion transference number is obtained [30]. Though GQD is used as an additive to improve the electrochemical property, but GQD is not investigated as an active electrode material for lithium ion and sodium ion batteries till date. It will be an interesting approach to utilize and investigate zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures as an active electrode material in secondary batteries. In order to utilize in rechargeable batteries, balance between performance and cost of production is required. But the production technique for the graphene quantum dots is tedious and time-consuming which increases the production cost. Till now, two fabrication approaches are attempted for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots, which are referred as top-down approach and bottom-up approach. Top-down approach refers to cutting down the graphene sheets into GQD such as chemical ablation [21], electrochemical oxidation, oxygen plasma treatment [31], using strong acids for the cleavage of the graphene sheets. These GQD have oxygen functional groups and are highly dispersible in water. Bottom-up techniques involve the formation of GQD from the molecular precursor and opening up of the fullerene. The bottom-up techniques, which are used currently for the synthesis, involve complicated procedures. Lili et al. synthesized graphene quantum dots using a chemical vapor deposition technique with the copper substrate as the catalyst where after the process of nucleation of graphene, the growth process is suppressed. The suppression of growth process is achieved by controlling the growth parameters like temperature and flow rate of precursor gas. By controlling the flow rate of methane gas the growth rate of graphene on copper foil is suppressed and the nucleation rate is increased. This helps to form graphene quantum dots rather than graphene [18]. Subodh et al. reported nitrogen doped GQD on a copper foil by chemical vapor deposition technique using chitosan as the carbon sources [32]. Though these reports present a new technique for the production of GQD, the production yield with these techniques is low such that it is not affordable to use in different applications. Even the top down approach followed involves the use of strong acids, organic solvents that are of multiple steps process and time-consuming. Sun et al. reported large-scale synthesis of graphene quantum dots using top-down approach. In this method, GQD is prepared by oxidation of graphite followed by the treatment of oxidized graphite with sodium carbonate. Then the solution is centrifuged to separate out the smaller size particles and then dialyzed using dialyzer bag for 3 days [33]. Though this method can be scaled up for the large-scale production, the method of production is time-consuming. Pan et al. developed a technique for the production of blue luminescent quantum dots using a hydrothermal route. Briefly, graphene nanosheets are treated with acids and undergone hydrothermal treatment followed by the use of dialysis bag to remove the larger sized particles [34]. This method of preparation also uses a dialysis bag, which again increases the production time. It will be of great interest if GQDs can be produced from a direct and scalable technique.

The present work reveals a new method of producing GQD and heteroatom doped GQD (N-GQD & B-GQD) in a single step process using bottom-up and top-down approach respectively. The chemical vapor deposition technique is considered as the scalable technique for the mass production of various carbon-based materials like carbon nanotubes and carbon fibers. Employing this scalable technique for the production of zero dimensional nanostructures will be of great interest [35]. So, we have employed chemical vapor deposition technique as the bottom-up approach for the synthesis of GQDs in a single step method. Large-scale, low temperature, cost-effective and single-step method without the use of dialysis bag for the preparation of B-GQD and N-GQD using graphite oxide and doping source has also been developed. The electrochemical lithium and sodium ion storage properties of as prepared GQD and heteroatom doped GQD alone are investigated in this study as contrast to previous studies wherein the GQDs are added as an performance booster [36]. Boron-doped GQDs exhibit a specific capacity of 1097mAh g−1and 310 mAh g−1at a specific current of 50 mA g−1 for lithium and sodium ion battery respectively. The cell exhibits cyclic stability of 500 cycles with a capacity retention of 95.7%. The developed method opens up a new technique for the synthesis of GQDs and doped GQDs in a large-scale production. As low-cost materials, the prepared GQD and heteroatom doped GQD represent a desirable candidate as anode materials for the large scale and stationary energy storage applications.

GQD was prepared by chemical vapor deposition technique. Misch metal based AB3 alloy MmNi3 was used as the catalyst for the growth of GQD. MmNi3was loaded into the quartz rube at the center of the tubular furnace. The temperature of the furnace is raised to 500 °C in the presence of argon atmosphere and once the temperature is reached H2gas is allowed for 30 min to remove the surface oxygen present in the catalyst. At 1050 °C, CH4 gas is allowed for 30 min and then the furnace is cooled down to room temperature. Further the purification of GQD is done to remove the amorphous carbon and the metal catalyst.

Graphite oxide (GO) was prepared by Hummer’s method [37]. B-GQD is obtained by mixing 1:1 ratio of GO and boric acid in DI water and dried at 60 °C to obtain the mixture. The mixture was then loaded at the center of the tubular furnace. At 400 °C, H2 gas is allowed for 30 min. The whole experiment is carried out in the argon atmosphere. The final product obtained is washed with warm water to remove the residual B2O3 and then dried at 60 °C to obtain B GQD.

N-GQD is obtained by mixing 1:1 ratio of GO and melamine in DI water and dried at 60 °C to obtain the mixture. The mixture was then loaded at the center of the tubular furnace. At 400 °C, H2 gas is allowed for 30 min. The whole experiment is carried out in the argon atmosphere. The final product obtained is N-GQD.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a Rigaku Smartlab X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). All measurements were taken in the range of 10–90° with the step size of 0.02°. Raman spectra were recorded using WiTec Alpha Raman Spectrometer in the range of 1000 to 3000 cm−1. Laser source used was Nd: YAG (532 nm). The thermal stability of the synthesized samples were characterized by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) at a heating 20 °C min−1 using SDT Q600 TA instruments. In order to study the morphology of the samples high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were recorded in Technai G20 instrument by drop casting sample solution in ethanol on holey carbon coated 200 mesh copper grids. X ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out in Specs X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with Mg Kα as the X-ray source and PHOIBOS 100MCD analyzer.

The slurry for the electrode was prepared by mixing 75% of active material, 10% of acetylene black and with 15% of sodium carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) in water. The working electrodes were prepared by coating slurry on the copper foil and then dried at 80 °C overnight in a vacuum oven. The dried electrodes were cut in the form of circular disc of 12 mm diameter. The loading of active material used for this study is 1 mg cm−2. 2032-coin cells were assembled in argon filled glove box where the oxygen and moisture levels were maintained below 0.1 ppm. Lithium/sodium metal was used as the counter electrode and reference electrode with glass fiber membrane (Whatman GF/C) as the separator. For lithium ion battery the electrolyte used was 1 M LiPF6 in EC: DMC. For sodium ion battery the electrolyte used was 1 M NaClO4 dissolved in ethylene carbonate propylene carbonate (PC) (EC: PC 1:1 v/v). The volume of the electrolyte used is 20 μl. All the electrochemical measurements are done using two-electrode half- cell configuration. The cells were galvanostatically cycled at various specific currents between 0.01 V and 2.8 V for sodium ion battery and 0.01 to 3.0 V for lithium-ion battery on Biologic BCS 810 battery cycler. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) were carried out on Biologic SP 300. The cyclic voltammetry was carried out in the potential range of 0.01 V–2.8 V and 0.01–3.0 V for sodium-ion and lithium ion batteries respectively at different scan rates from 0.1 to 1 mV/s. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements are carried out in Biologic VSP 300 in the frequency range of 10 mHz to 1 MHz at a voltage amplitude of 10 mV. Electrode density of working electrodes was estimated by calculating the volume of the sample coated layer without considering mass and volume the current collector and additives. Electrode densities of B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD anodes are 0.811, 0.73 and 0.66 g ml−1 respectively. Average discharge voltage for LIB anode was calculated by integrating the galvanostatic discharge profiles and divided by specific gravimetric capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of undoped and heteroatom doped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Schematic for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots and heteroatom doped graphene quantum dots are shown in Fig. 1 . The synthesis process for GQD has been attempted using chemical vapor deposition technique. Misch metal-based nickel alloy is used as the catalyst for the growth of GQD and the catalyst loaded in a quartz boat is subjected to the reaction zone of the tubular furnace. At 1050 °C, the decomposition of methane gas causes deposition of carbon on the surface of catalyst. After the process of nucleation of carbon on the surface of the catalyst particles, the growth occurs in the form of sheets. After the process of removal of catalyst particles, pure GQD is obtained. For the preparation of B-GQD, the mixture of boric acid and graphite oxide are taken as the initial materials. Boric acid acts as the source of boron doping and also helps to form quantum dots by scissoring the graphite oxide. Since the synthesis procedure has been carried out at a low temperature of 400 °C, boron doped graphene is not formed which takes place at a higher temperature of 900–1200 °C [38]. The presence of functional groups in graphite oxide facilitates the formation of GQD by tuning the carbonization of the precursor. For the preparation of N-GQD, melamine acts as the source of nitrogen doping as well as the agent for the formation of graphene quantum dots. The complete decomposition of melamine occurs at a temperature of 800 °C and the incomplete carbonization of melamine at a temperature of 400 °C leads to the formation of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots. The formation of nitrogen doped quantum dots depends mainly on the reaction temperature. When the temperature is increased beyond 600 °C, no quantum dots are formed and it results in the formation of nitrogen doped graphene [39]. This is evident from the TEM images as shown in Fig. S1a wherein wrinkled graphene nanosheets are observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical characterization of GQD, B-GQD and N-GQD
ce:section.ce:para:
The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of GQD, B-GQD and N-GQD show a broad peak at 25.3°, 26.6° and 24.8° corresponding to (0 0 2) graphitic carbon peak for GQD, N-GQD and B-GQD respectively (Fig. 2 a). The broadness in the peak is due to the smaller size of the quantum dots [33,40]. Also, from the XRD pattern, it is confirmed that the formed quantum dots are crystalline in nature. It is observed that in B-GQD, the peak is shifted to a lower angle, which may be due to the introduction of boron into the graphitic structure leading to an increase in interlayer spacing. The atomic radius of nitrogen atom (65 pm) is less than the size of carbon atom (70 pm). So the doping of nitrogen in the carbon lattice does not interrupt the structure of carbon and hence there will not be enhancement in interlayer spacing. But the doping of nitrogen in the carbon lattice alters the electronic structure due to the presence of lone pair of electrons. So, the peak shift to higher angle in N-GQD could be attributed to the
π - π
stacking of graphene with structural defects created by nitrogen doping. Doping of heteroatoms like boron with larger size and smaller electronegativity than carbon can enhance the interlayer spacing. Also, it is observed from XPS study, the deconvoluted peak of B 1 s shows the presence of BC2O. The presence of strong chemical bond between boron and oxygen helps to enlarge the interlayer spacing [41]. So, the peak shift observed in B-GQD is due to the enhancement in interlayer spacing. Raman spectra of GQD, B-GQD and N-GQD show the characteristic G band and D band (Fig. 2b). The G band centered at 1332.9 cm−1, 1319 cm−1 and 1325 cm−1 respectively for GQD, B-GQD and N-GQD indicates the graphitic nature of the carbon structure and is mainly due to the E2g vibrational mode of CC bond stretching. The downshift of G band in doped graphene quantum dots when compared to graphene quantum dots suggest the fact that graphene quantum dots are doped with n-type dopants [42].The D band centered at 1586 cm−1, 1568 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1 respectively for GQD, B-GQD and NGQD is due to the creation of structural defects. The ID/IG ratio for GQD, N-GQD and B-GQD are 1.15, 1.04 and 1.15 respectively is higher and are arising from the edge defects present in the GQD [40,43,44] The thermal stability of graphene quantum dots is determined using thermogravimetric analysis in air atmosphere. It is observed from the graph as shown in Fig. S2 that GQD is stable up to a temperature of 800 °C and N-GQD, B-GQD are stable up to a temperature of 600 °C. B-GQD and N-GQD decompose at a lower temperature than GQD due to the presence of defects introduced by the dopant atoms.

Morphological analysis of the synthesized samples GQD, B-GQD and N-GQD are studied by TEM images. Fig. 3 a and b illustrate TEM images of GQD with a uniform distribution. Fig. 3c represents TEM image of B-GQD where the graphene quantum dots are homogeneously distributed. The magnified TEM image of B-GQD (Fig. 3d) reveals that the boron doped quantum dots are of almost same size (highlighted in red circle) in the entire region. In the case of N-GQD, TEM image (Fig. 3e and f) clearly shows that the quantum dots are supported and uniformly distributed. The higher resolution TEM image of N-GQD in Fig. S2b shows the quantum dots are highly crystalline in nature (marked in red circles) as consistent from the XRD studies (Fig. 2a). To obtain further insight about boron and nitrogen doping in graphene quantum dots as well as the chemical composition of B-GQD and N-GQD, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies have been done. Fig. S3a & c represent survey spectra of B-GQD and N-GQD and the survey spectrum of B-GQD consists of O1s, C1s and B1s peaks and no other impurities are identified. In order to understand the nature of bonding in B-GQD, the higher resolution spectra of C1s, O1s and B1s are deconvoluted after subtraction of Shirley background. C1s spectrum of B-GQD illustrated in Fig. 4 a is fitted into four peaks corresponding to CC (284.4 eV), CO (285.39 eV), COC (286.57 eV), CO (288.17 eV).[45] O1s is fitted into three peaks as COO− (531.7 eV), CO (533.22), COH (534.6 eV) (Fig. S3b). B 1s spectrum of B-GQD represented in Fig. 4b is deconvoluted into two peaks as BC3 (191.1 eV), BC2O (192.55 eV) which confirms the boron doping into the carbon matrix [46]. The bonding between boron and carbon indicates that boron is doped into the carbon structure. This helps to enhance the interlayer spacing, which is consistent with the XRD data where peak shift to lower angle is obtained (Fig. 2a). This enhanced interlayer facilitates better sodium and lithium ion insertion thereby helps to enhance the specific capacity. The survey spectrum of N-GQD (Fig. S3c) consists of three high intense peaks at 284.0, 399.9 and 530.8 eV corresponding to C1s, N 1s, and O1s respectively. Nitrogen content quantified from XPS survey spectrum of N-GQD is found to be 16 at%. In the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 4(c)) the peak at 285.6 eV corresponds to CN bonding, the other peaks at 284.6 eV, 287.9 eV, 292.1 eV signifies the CC, CO, OCO bonds respectively [47]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s (Fig. 4d) is deconvoluted into four peaks pyridinic N (398.5 eV), pyrrolic N (399.4 eV) graphitic N (400.2 eV) NH (401.3 eV) [48,49] with high pyridinic N component. The pyridinic N and graphitic N improve the electrochemical activity of the carbon matrix and thereby enhance lithium and sodium storage capacity. The O1s high-resolution spectra (Fig. S3d) was deconvoluted into two peaks at 532.4 eV, 533.6 eV as CO, OCO bonds respectively [50].

Fig. S4 shows the photoluminescence (PL) studies of B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD at different excitation wavelengths. The excitation wavelength of B-GQD is varied from 230 to 360 nm and the emission occurs at 550 nm for all the excitation wavelengths (Fig. S4a). This suggests that B-GQD emission wavelength is independent of excitation wavelength. Similarly, as shown in Fig. S4b N-GQD PL spectra is obtained at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 310 to 350 nm with a wavelength independent emission at 410 nm. Fig. S4c represents the PL spectra of GQD for various excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 370 nm. PL spectra of GQD also suggest its wavelength independent with the emission at 550 nm. Moreover, B-GQD and GQD show the maximum emission wavelength at 550 nm with 350 nm excitation wavelength whereas, N GQD shows maximum emission wavelength at 410 nm with an excitation wavelength at 350 nm. The wavelength independent emission for B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD samples indicate that the florescence occurs due to the uniform distribution of quantum dots [50] which are consistent with TEM images.

The interaction of lithium ions with GQD, B-GQD and N-GQD are studied by cyclic voltammetry and galvanostatic charge-discharge measurements. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) has been carried out at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 in the potential range of 0.01–3 V. Fig. 5 a represents first three CV profiles of B-GQD. In the first cycle, a sharp reduction peak at 0.3 V depicts irreversible decomposition of electrolyte on the surface of the anode and formation of solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer. In addition, the redox peak around 0 V represents the insertion and de-insertion of Li+ ions into carbonaceous materials [51]. The absence of the reduction peak at 0.3 V in the further cycles suggests the formation of the stable SEI layer in the first cycle. The overlapping of CV profile in second and third cycles attributes to the good stability of B-GQD anode towards Li+ ions insertion and de-insertion. N-GQD and GQD anodes also exhibit similar CV profiles as shown in Fig. S5a and b.

Galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles of B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD are obtained by cycling the cell in the potential range of 0.01 to 3.0 V. Fig. 5b depicts the charge-discharge curves of B-GQD anode cycled at a specific current of 50 mA g−1 for the first three cycles. The first discharge cycle of B-GQD anode delivers a specific capacity of 1896 mA h g−1 with the plateau region observed at 0.8 V, due to the formation SEI layer on the anode, which is consistent with CV results. The reversible specific capacity obtained for B-GQD after SEI layer formation is 1097 mA h g−1. The first and second cycle discharge capacities of N-GQD are 1500 and 770 mA h g−1 respectively whereas GQD shows 697 and 479 mA h g−1 for the first and second discharge capacity at 50 mA g−1 respectively as shown in Fig. S6a and b. The capacity loss is observed for the first and the second discharge curves of B-GQD, NGQD and GQD anodes. The loss in capacity is due to the formation of SEI layer which in turn depends on the surface of electrode materials. Since graphene quantum dots have high surface area compared to low surface area carbon based materials, huge amount of lithium is consumed in the first cycle for the SEI layer formation. In order to utilize the graphene quantum dots for practical study, pre-lithiation strategies can be adopted which can leads to the scope of future work. Compared to undoped GQD, boron and nitrogen doped GQD show higher specific capacity in the initial cycles. This is because B and N dopant atoms create electron deficient and electron rich sites in carbon lattice respectively, which enhances the Li ion adsorption and storage capacity.[52] Galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles for B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD at different specific currents are shown in Fig. S6c, d and e. The charge-discharge profiles of B-GQD and N-GQD have a similar pattern at different specific currents consisting of the sloping region indicating the storage of Li+ ions in dopant sites. However, charge-discharge profile of GQD (Fig. S6e) resembles the Li+ ions storage in carbonaceous materials.

Fig. 5c illustrates the rate performance of GQD, B-GQD and N-GQD anode for LIB at different specific currents ranging from 50 mA g−1 to 1.5 A g−1. The specific capacities obtained for GQD at various specific currents like 50 mA g−1, 100 mA g−1, 200 mA g−1, 500 mA g−1, 1 and 1.5 A g−1 are 470, 441, 393, 356, 322 and 306mAh g−1 respectively. N-GQD exhibits specific capacities of 740, 499, 428, 386, 322 and 293mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1, 100 mA g−1, 200 mA g−1, 500 mA g−1, 1 and 1.5 A g−1 respectively. The specific capacity delivered by N-GQD at lower specific current is higher than GQD. The enhanced Li ion storage capacity of N-GQD is attributed to nitrogen doping- sites in N-GQD which causes defects in the graphene structure thereby helps Li+ ion storage properties. In addition, the presence of high amount of pyridinic N in N-GQD contributes to enhancement of the reversible capacity as the pyridinic N acts as electron donor which can bind Li+ ion very strongly [53]. Similarly, B-GQD delivers specific capacity of 859, 663, 507, 460, 359, 350 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1, 100 mA g−1, 200 mA g−1, 500 mA g−1, 1 and 1.5 A g−1 respectively. The coulombic efficiency values for the rate capability tests of B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD Li-ion battery anodes are given in Fig. S7. The specific gravimetric and volumetric capacities as well as average discharge voltages of B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD anodes for Li-ion battery at different cycles and specific currents are tabulated in Table1 . B-GQD delivers higher specific capacity than N-GQD and GQD at all specific currents. After cycling at higher specific current and cycling back to lower specific current of 50 mA g−1, B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD exhibit the specific capacity of 660, 521, 400 mAh g−1 respectively which is 88%, 65% and 83% of their initial capacity. The long-term cyclic ability of B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD are tested by maintaining a constant specific current of 200 mA g−1 for 500 cycles and illustrated in Fig. 5d. B-GQD as LIB anode is able to retain 95.7% of its initial capacity after 500 cycles whereas N-GQD and GQD maintain 90 and 86% of their initial capacity. All three LIB anodes show coulombic efficiency of almost 100% over 500 cycles. The superior performance of the B-GQD and N-GQD signifies the role of dopant atoms in enhancing Li+ ion storage capacity. Many theoretical studies have proved that boron doping makes the graphene electron deficient system and the Li+ ion storage capacity increases at these sites. Moreover, one boron atom doping into the carbon matrix can absorb six Li+ ions which makes it an efficient material for lithium storage [54].

Insight into unveiling the lithium charge storage process in B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD anodes, cyclic voltammetry measurements are taken at different scan rates from 0.1 to 1 mV s−1 in potential range 0.01 to 3 V as shown in Fig. 6 a–c. The relationship between the peak current and scan rate is given equation (1).
(1)
i = a
v
b
where a and b are adjustable constants and the value of b determined by the slope of log(i)-log(v) curves and it signifies the lithium charge storage process. If the value of b is 0.5 then the electrochemical reaction is an ideal linear diffusion-controlled process and if the value of b is 1, it represents a surface-controlled process [55–57]. The linear relationship between log(i)-log(v) curves for B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD are shown in Fig. 6d. The b value determined from Fig. 6d for B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD are 0.6, 1.0, and 0.7 respectively. For N-GQD, the b value around 1 indicates the lithium charge storage process is a surface-controlled process. As the lithium insertion/de-insertions reactions in N-GQD are surface-controlled process that is the reactions occur only on the interphase of the electrolyte and the surface of the electrode rather than diffusion which can increase the thickness of SEI layer leads to irreversible capacity. This might be a reason at the higher specific current, the specific capacity of N-GQD is less than GQD. Moreover, as reported in literature, the nitrogen doped carbon nanomaterials delivers much higher specific capacities due to strong interaction between the nitrogen doped carbon sites and Li+ ions. As the nitrogen at% in the carbon matrix increases more than 10 at%, it causes the structural instability which reduces the overall performance of the Li-ion battery [58,59]. In the present work, the high nitrogen content (16 at%) in N-GQD originates the carbon structure instability during Li+ ion insertion/de-insertion and leads to the irreversible capacity loss upon cycling. In the case of B-GQD and GQD, both diffusion and surface-controlled process occurs which helps to attain higher rate capability and specific capacity. Overall, B-GQD has shown superior performance in lithium ion storage than N-GQD and GQD anodes.

The behavior of sodium insertion and deinsertion in graphene quantum dots and doped graphene quantum dots are studied using various electrochemical measurements. Initially, cyclic voltammetry measurements are carried out in the potential range of 0.01–2.8 V at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 for three cycles as shown in Fig. 7 a. The initial cycle shows a high-intensity cathodic peak at 0.01 V due to the decomposition of the electrolyte resulting in the irreversible formation of solid electrolyte interphase layer (SEI). This peak is not observed in the further cycles due to the irreversible and stable formation of SEI layer. In addition, a broad redox peak is observed from 0.5 V to 0.01 V, which indicates the reversible insertion and de-insertion of Na+ ions into the B-GQD structure (Fig. 7a). The doping creates defects in the structure, which helps for the reversible adsorption of sodium ions. As it can be seen from the Fig. S8a, GQD and N GQD also show a similar profile, which is consistent with the typical carbon-based material.

The galvanostatic charge-discharge measurements were carried out in the potential range of 0.01 V to 2.8 V. The charge-discharge profile of B GQD in the first three cycles is shown in Fig. 7b. The first cycle shows a plateau at 0.6 V, which is due to the formation of SEI layer as a result of electrolyte decomposition, which is consistent with the CV result. The charge discharge profile in the first cycle shows a sloping region as well as a plateau region. In the case of doped GQD, the capacity contribution from the sloping region is higher than that of the plateau region. This is due to the fact that sodium ions adsorb more to the doped sites as well as insertion of sodium ions into the carbon structure [60]. But in the case of GQD, the capacity contribution from the sloping region is very less compared to the plateau region (Fig. S9c). So, the storage of sodium ions in GQD occurs by the adsorption of sodium ions onto the carbon structure. This could be the reason for the less specific capacity obtained for GQD.

As for the rate performance, the cell was cycled at different specific currents from 50 mA g−1 to 2 A g−1. GQD exhibits the specific capacities of 156 mAh g−1, 113 mAh g−1, 102 mAh g−1, 79 mAh g−1, 71 mAh g−1, 64 mAh g−1, 51 mAh g−1and 39 mAh g−1 at different specific currents like 50 mA g−1,100 mA g−1, 200 mA g−1, 500 mA g−1, 800 mA g−1, 1 A g−1, 1.5 A g−1 and 2 A g−1 respectively. Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots shows specific capacities of 215 mAh g−1, 188 mAh g−1, 163 mAh g−1, 135 mAh g−1, 100 mA h g−1, 82 mAh g−1, 72 mA h g−1 at 50 mA g−1, 100 mA g−1, 200 mA g−1, 500 mA g−1, 800 mA g−1, 1 A g−1, 1.5 A g−1 and 2 A g−1 respectively. The enhancement in specific capacity is obtained with nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots when compared to GQD. The enhancement in specific capacity is about 37.8% at a specific current of 50 mA g−1. Nitrogen doping in the carbon structure alters the electronic conductivity and electrochemical performance [61–64]. B-GQD exhibits better rate capability when compared to N GQD and GQD. N- GQD exhibit the specific capacities of 307 mAh g−1, 266 mAh g−1, 224 mAh g−1, 173 mAh g−1, 141 mAh g−1, 126 mAh g−1, 109 mAh g−1, 85 mAh g−1at 50 mA g−1, 100 mA g−1, 200 mA g−1, 500 mA g−1, 800 mA g−1, 1 A g−1, 1.5 A g−1 and 2 A g−1 respectively. When the specific current is turned back to 50 mA g−1, the reversible capacity is recovered up to 269 mAh g−1. The enhancement in the specific capacity obtained for BGQD is about 96.7% and 41.4% when compared to GQD and N-GQD respectively. The boron doping increases the number of in plane local defects, which also facilitates for the sodium ion storage. It has been reported in a computational study that boron doping enhances the electron mobility and sodium ion mobility [65]. The enhanced sodium ion mobility in BGQD helps to obtain better rate capability when compared to GQD and N-GQD. The coulombic efficiency for the rate capability curve is shown in Fig. S10 and it is observed to be close to 100%. Even at higher specific current, the coulombic efficiency is remains at 100% which signifies the reversibility of the anode material.

The volumetric capacity values are highly important when analyzing the anode material for the practical application [66,67]. Hence volumetric capacity of GQD, B-GQD and N-GQD are calculated from the density of the electrode. The volumetric capacity of the electrode at different specific currents is listed in Table 2 .

To further elucidate the sodium ion storage performance, the cell is cycled at 200 mA g−1 for long-term cycling. As shown in Fig. 7d B-GQD, N-GQD and GQD retain the specific capacity of 96 mAh g−1, 106 mAh g−1 and 72 mAh g−1 with the capacity retention of 53%, 66% and 75% respectively at the end of 500 cycles. The better rate capability is obtained for N-GQD and B-GQD and good capacity retention is obtained for GQD. The introduction of heteroatom helps to alter the electronic property of the carbon structure and helps in adsorption of sodium ions into the structure. These change in property of the material helps to attain better rate capability. Also, the coulombic efficiency is stabilized at 100% over the entire 500 cycles. It is observed that capacity fade for sodium ion battery performance is more when compared to lithium ion battery. The formation of stable solid electrolyte interphase layer is responsible for the stable cycling of the cell which in turn depends on the extent of decomposition of electrolyte solvent. The decomposition of electrolyte solvent can be reduced by adding electrolyte additives like fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) which prevents the electrolyte decomposition and helps in the formation of stable SEI layer. The cycling performance of sodium ion battery using GQD is very stable as compared to lithium ion battery performance. But the cycling performance of N-GQD obtained at the end of 500 cycles is about 66%. Similar nature of capacity fading is also observed in hard carbon anode in sodium ion battery without the electrolyte additive and improvement in capacity retention is obtained using electrolyte additive [68]. So, the cycling performance of GQD anode in sodium ion battery can be improved by adding additives into the electrolyte which thereby forms a stable electrolyte interphase layer and can leads to stable capacity retention.

In order to gain further insights into the kinetics of the reaction, CV at different scan rates from 0.1 to 1 mV s−1 are done as shown in Fig. 8 a–c. It is observed from the Fig. 8a–c that with the increase in the scan rate, the peak current increases along with the shift in the peak voltage. The relationship between the peak current and scan rate can be illustrated using the following expression as given in Eq. (1).

The value of constant b obtained for GQD, B GQD and N GQD are 0.7, 0.9 and 1 respectively. The value of constant b is close to 1 indicating the process is predominantly controlled by a surface-controlled process. In the case of GQD and B GQD, the value of b indicates that it has both surface controlled and diffusion-controlled process. Boron doping causes an increase in interlayer spacing, which helps to enhance insertion of sodium ions into the carbon structure. The surface-controlled process is due to the edge defects present on the GQDs where the sodium ions are physically adsorbed onto the surface. The presence of both surfaces controlled and diffusion-controlled process helps to attain good rate capability and cyclic stability.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique is also carried out to determine the impedance offered by the fabricated cell. The EIS measurements were recorded after CV measurements followed by the rest period of 2 h. Electrochemical impedance spectra of GQD, N-GQD and B-GQD anodes for Li-ion battery are shown in the Fig. S11a. The Nyquist plot of all the three anodes consists of two semicircles in high frequency and mid frequency corresponds to the resistance for SEI layer formation and charge transfer resistance respectively. The sloping region corresponds to Warburg resistance (W2). The EIS profile were fitted using the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. S11a inset. The R1, R2, R3 represents the Ohmic, SEI and charge-transfer resistance respectively and Q1 and Q2 corresponds to the constant phase elements. The resistance offered during the formation of SEI (R2) for GQD, N-GQD and B-GQD anodes in Li-ion battery are 40 Ω, 105 Ω, and 81 Ω respectively. Compared to GQD, N-GQD and B-GQD shows higher SEI layer resistance as the boron and nitrogen dopants sites in the carbon absorbs more Li+ ions during the SEI layer formation.

The Nyquist plot for sodium ion cell is shown in Fig. S11b. and the semicircle represents the charge transfer resistance and the sloping region represents the Warburg resistance corresponding to the diffusion of lithium/sodium ion from the bulk of the electrolyte to the surface of the electrode. The charge transfer resistance is the resistance offered by the cell due to the formation of solid electrolyte interphase layer between the electrode and electrolyte. The low resistance offered by the cell implies better ionic and electrical conductivity of the fabricated system. All the samples does not show Warburg like behaviour (α = 0.5). For the sodium ion storage, the respective values of α for GQD, N-GQD and B-GQD are 0.92, 0.56 and 0.76 (Fig. S11b). In the case of GQD, capacitive nature of charge storage occurs and in N-GQD and B-GQD the charge storage occurs by the process of physical adsorption as well as diffusion of sodium ions. The charge transfer resistance of GQD, N-GQD and B-GQD for lithium and sodium ion batteries are tabulated in Table 3 .

To summarize, we demonstrated a simple and scalable technique for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots and B & N doped quantum dots with high yield. The prepared graphene quantum dots are homogeneously distributed as observed from the TEM images. Rather than optoelectronic property of quantum dots, electrochemical property of the GQDs, B-GQD and N-GQDs in lithium and sodium ion battery are illustrated. The potential use of doped and undoped graphene quantum dots as an anode material for the secondary battery is realized for the first time in this study. Boron doped GQD (B-GQD) exhibits the highest specific capacity of 1097 mAh g−1 and 310 mAh g−1 at a specific current of 50 mA g−1 for lithium and sodium ion batteries respectively. Also, the cell observes a satisfactory cyclic performance for 500 cycles with good capacity retention. B-GQD exhibits high volumetric energy density of 537 Ah L−1 and 214 Ah L−1 with an average voltage of 0.43 V and 0.57 respectively for lithium ion and sodium ion batteries. The presence of edge defects in quantum dots helps to adsorb more metal ions and doping helps to enhance the insertion process, which is responsible for its impressive specific capacity among the carbon-based materials. This study thus provides a new method for the development of zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures and provides a new scope for the development of new type of anode materials for lithium ion and sodium ion batteries.
